name: Visa / Boarding-Denied Recovery — When the Gate Agent Is Wrong
description: A gate agent has refused to board you over a visa, passport, or entry-rule issue. They are sometimes right and often wrong (TIMATIC misread, transit-rule confusion, passport-validity overcautious). This skill walks through verifying entry rules, pushing back politely, escalating to the duty manager, and rerouting via another carrier if denial holds.
disclaimer: Final entry decisions are made by the destination country's border authorities, not by airlines. This skill helps reverse erroneous AIRLINE denials based on misreads of standard sources like TIMATIC/Sherpa. Always carry the actual entry-rule text printed out for the destination country.

context.when_to_use: User is at check-in or the gate, has been told they cannot board because of a visa, passport-validity, transit-visa, or ESTA/eTA issue. User believes (with documentation) that the entry rule allows the trip.
preconditions: User has the passport, any required visa, and the trip's onward/return documentation.
User can pull up the destination government's official immigration page from a phone.
User has time before the flight closes (this conversation takes 20-40 minutes typically).

principles: Airlines use TIMATIC (a Sabre / IATA database) and increasingly Sherpa to determine boarding eligibility. The data is sometimes stale or misread. The destination government's own site is the source of truth.

Airlines are financially liable for transporting passengers refused entry — they're the ones who pay the return-flight cost and INAD (inadmissible passenger) fines, often $5,000-$15,000+. That's why they're cautious. Address that fear directly: 'I can show you the rule that confirms I'm admissible.'

Passport-validity rules trip up more travelers than any other. Common standards: 6 months beyond travel for most of Asia + Schengen; 3 months beyond travel for some; valid for trip duration for others (UK, US). Check the destination, not the assumption.

Transit visa confusion is the second biggest source of erroneous denials. Schengen requires nothing for most airside transit, but a few nationalities need an 'Airport Transit Visa' (ATV / Visa A). UK requires a Direct Airside Transit Visa (DATV) for many nationalities even airside. China offers 24/72/144-hour visa-free transit at specific ports.
The duty manager is the person who can override a gate agent on TIMATIC interpretation. Ask for them by title, calmly.

If denial holds, your options are: rebook through a different routing where you're clearly admissible, return home and apply for the proper visa, or visit the destination embassy. The airline must refund a denied-boarding ticket if the denial was their error.

phrases.opener: I understand you're seeing an issue. Can we walk through the entry rule together? I have the destination government's own page on my phone showing I'm admissible.
phrases.request_timatic_recheck: Could you re-pull TIMATIC for my passport country [code] traveling to [destination], with my onward ticket and visa type [type]? I think the entry being shown may not match my exact case.
phrases.show_official_source: Here's the [destination country] immigration page — it says [exact rule]. My situation matches because [reasons]. The TIMATIC summary may be outdated.
phrases.ask_for_duty_manager: Could you ask your station duty manager or supervisor to take a second look? I'm not trying to push past you — I want their eyes on the rule too.
phrases.address_airline_risk: I understand you're concerned about an INAD fine. The rule itself says I'm admissible. If you'd like, I can sign a waiver acknowledging I take responsibility — but the rule should hold.
phrases.ask_for_written_denial: If we can't move forward, can you give me a written denied-boarding notice citing the specific rule? I'll need it for the refund and to plan the next step.
phrases.escalate_offsite: If your team is firm here, I'd like to call [destination country] embassy/consulate. They can sometimes provide a clarification letter in real time.
phrases.graceful_close_success: Thank you for taking the time to recheck. Could you put a note on my PNR that this was resolved at [station] so I don't hit the same issue on rebooking or return?
phrases.graceful_close_failure: I understand. Please give me the written denial and process the refund per your involuntary denied-boarding policy.

tactics.name: Pull the destination's official immigration page
tactics.script: On your phone, open the destination country's immigration / foreign-affairs site. US: travel.state.gov. UK: gov.uk/check-uk-visa. Schengen: ec.europa.eu/home-affairs. Singapore: ica.gov.sg. China: nia.gov.cn. Show the exact text of the rule that covers your case. Government source > TIMATIC summary.

tactics.name: Ask for a TIMATIC recheck with full inputs
tactics.script: TIMATIC's answer depends on inputs: passport-issuing country, destination, layover countries, visa held, onward ticket. A miss on any input gives the wrong answer. Politely ask the agent to re-enter all inputs out loud so you can verify together.

tactics.name: Address the airline's INAD exposure
tactics.when: Agent is hesitant but not refusing.
tactics.script: Acknowledge their risk: 'I know if I'm denied at the border you're stuck with the return and a fine.' Then point at the rule again. Agents soften when you name the fear they can't say aloud.

tactics.name: Escalate to the duty manager
tactics.when: Gate agent has made a firm call and shift won't budge.
tactics.script: By title: 'station duty manager', 'shift supervisor', 'gate supervisor'. Not 'manager' (vague). Be calm — duty managers will side with the agent if you've been hostile.

tactics.name: Call the destination embassy or consulate
tactics.when: Time permits and the denial is borderline.
tactics.script: Many embassies issue real-time clarification letters or 24/7 calls for traveler emergencies. Examples that have done so: Singapore ICA, UK Home Office, German Auswärtiges Amt. The agent will often accept embassy confirmation by phone.

tactics.name: Reroute through a different transit point
tactics.when: Denial is specifically about transit, not destination.
tactics.script: Example: UK DATV issue → reroute via DUB (Ireland), CDG (France), or non-stop. Schengen transit visa issue → reroute via DOH, DXB, IST, or non-stop. The destination is still reachable; just not via this routing.

tactics.name: Document everything
tactics.script: If denied: written denial, agent name, time, station, your boarding pass, the rule text you presented. If accepted after escalation: note on PNR. Both protect you on the return flight or future trips.

boundaries: Do NOT raise your voice or threaten — boarding denial can escalate to a no-fly note on your record.
Do NOT lie about your itinerary, return ticket, or visa status. Border systems share data; getting caught is worse than being denied.
Do NOT board with a borderline case if you're not confident — getting refused at destination border means being returned at your cost and a possible multi-year entry ban.
Do NOT assume the gate agent is wrong; they're usually right. Verify before pushing.
Do NOT skip getting the written denial if you fail — it's required for refund and insurance.
success_signals: Agent re-runs TIMATIC and the new result allows boarding.
Duty manager reads the official rule and approves.
Embassy confirms over the phone and the airline accepts.
Agent issues a boarding pass with a note explaining the override.
failure_signals: Both gate agent AND duty manager firmly stand on the denial.
The official rule is genuinely ambiguous or has been recently changed.
Time runs out (flight closes) before resolution.
Embassy / consulate is unreachable (weekend / holiday in destination country).

exit_strategy.on_success: Board, but bring printed copies of the rule + the conversation notes — you may need to repeat the conversation on the return leg.
exit_strategy.on_failure: Get the written denial and the refund. Decide: apply for the proper visa and rebook, reroute via a permissive transit, or cancel the trip and claim insurance trip-cancellation (if applicable).
follow_ups: If the denial was wrong, file a written complaint with the airline citing the rule + TIMATIC misread. Refunds and rebookings have been granted retroactively.
If you suspect the issue will recur (e.g. you fly this route monthly), email the airline's Special Services / INAD desk a clarification request so the system updates.
If denied entry was at a foreign border (not by the airline), that's a different document trail — keep all paperwork for future visa applications which will ask about prior denials.

required_user_info: Passport country and validity expiry date
Destination country and trip purpose (tourism, transit, business, study, work)
Length of stay and onward ticket details
Visa or ETA held (type, validity, entry count remaining)
The exact reason the gate agent stated for denial
